summary: Gruber addresses concerns about Uber's iOS app switching from 'When using the app' to 'Always' location tracking. While acknowledging Michael S. Fischer's alarm and his own deep skepticism of Uber, Gruber points out that iOS already provides a practical way to verify Uber's claims. By checking Settings → Privacy → Location Services, users can see whether Uber has accessed their location recently. Gruber reports that after monitoring this for weeks, he has found no evidence of misuse. He concludes that while distrust of Uber is warranted, the evidence shows they are doing what they claim in this specific case.
key_points: Uber changed its iOS location tracking to 'Always' but claims it only tracks for five minutes after a ride ends to improve drop-off accuracy
iOS's Location Services settings provide a built-in audit trail showing when apps have recently accessed location data (purple for recent, gray for last 24 hours)
After weeks of personal monitoring, Gruber found no evidence that Uber is misusing the 'Always' location privilege
While iOS lacks the fine-grained location controls Fischer requests, existing tools are sufficient to verify Uber's specific claims
Gruber maintains deep skepticism of Uber as a company while acknowledging the evidence in this particular case
money_quotes: Few people are more skeptical about Uber than I am.
I don't trust Uber. But we can collectively verify that in this case, they're doing exactly what they say they're doing.

iOS does not give users the fine-grained control over apps' location-tracking privileges that Fischer is asking for, but it does give us a way to verify that Uber is only using its 'Always' privilege the way it claims to be.
I've been checking this every few days ever since Uber changed its location-checking privilege, and it has never once shown any sign of misuse.
key_insight: Rather than defaulting to outrage over Uber's location-tracking change, iOS already provides the tools to empirically verify whether the company is abusing the privilege — and in this case, it isn't.
